text: The surgeon inserts the reusable transanal D-port into the anus and removes the obturator, preparing the surgical field for the endolaparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon applies a reusable silicone cap to the transanal D-port and introduces a regular thirty-degree, ten-millimeter scope into the surgical field for visualization.
A monocurve reusable grasping forceps is inserted through the nine o'clock port orifice of the transanal D-port, visible within the rectal lumen.
A graduated straight grasping forceps is inserted through the three o'clock port orifice during the transanal endolaparoscopic resection procedure.
The surgeon observes the degenerated rectal polyp located less than two centimeters from the dentate line using a transanal endolaparoscopic approach.
A monocurve reusable coagulating hook is inserted through the three o'clock port orifice, visible within the transanal surgical field.
The surgeon uses a coagulating hook to apply circumferential scores around the lesion, ensuring safe margins are maintained.

The surgeon begins the resection at the six o'clock position using a monocurved grasping forceps and a monocurved coagulating hook, targeting the area around the degenerated polyp near the dentic line.
The surgeon incises the mucosal and submucosal layers, followed by sectioning the muscular layers, which exposes the recto-rectal space.
The surgeon performs dissection on the left lateral side of the rectal tissue, continuing the resection process with visible manipulation of the tissue layers.
The surgeon visualizes the correct plane of dissection within the recto-rectal space, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon continues dissection on the left lateral side, incising the remnant rectal wall with precision.
The surgeon continues the dissection in the upper part of the rectal area using a monocurved coagulating hook, advancing through the tissue layers.
The surgeon initiates dissection on the right lateral side of the rectal wall using the full thickness method, carefully separating tissue layers.

The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of the perirectal fatty tissue, revealing the rectal muscular layer, submucosal layer, and mucosal layers, with clear visualization of each anatomical structure.
The surgeon removes the completely freed rectal specimen through the D-port, preparing it for pathological evaluation.
The surgeon selects a monocled reusable needle holder and inserts an absorbable barbed suture into the surgical field.
The surgeon inserts the monocled reusable needle holder into the three o'clock port orifice, preparing for suturing.
The surgeon closes the rectal wall by suturing, beginning at the central point and proceeding to the left lateral side.
The surgeon places the first sutures on the dentate line to address the retraction of the sectioned tissue.
The surgeon places full-thickness sutures in the rectal wall, ensuring the suture is kept under tension to maintain closure.
The surgeon continues suturing the rectal wall, reaching the middle segment of the left lateral side, and takes subsequent full thickness bites to secure the tissue.
The surgeon ties a final intercorporeal knot to secure the suture on the rectal wall.
Monocurve reusable scissors are inserted through the three o'clock port orifice, and the surgeon cuts the first suture with precision.
The surgeon begins a new reabsorbable barbed suture at the apex of the right lateral dissection, indicating the start of a new suturing sequence.
The surgeon begins closure of the rectal wall using a full thickness suturing technique, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue layers.
The surgeon places subsequent full thickness suture bites along the rectal wall closure.
The suture is advanced to the central point where the previous suture was initiated, aligning with the earlier closure technique.
The surgeon creates an intercorporeal knot to secure the suture at the site of the rectal wall closure.
The final view of the surgical cavity reveals the completed suture line along the rectal wall, with no visible openings or flaps remaining.
